# SRE agent GitHub integration and security (2025-12-07 11:41Z)

_**User (2025-12-07 11:41Z)**_

Here’s what happened in the E2E run of the SRE agent:

Slack: ✅ Message posted (I saw it).

GitHub: ❌ Failed to create the issue. GitHub MCP logs show “Resource not accessible by personal access token”.

Log snippet:

github-1 | Failed to create issue: Resource not accessible by personal access token

github-1 | Failed to create issue: Resource not accessible by personal access token

Logs/code check: Loki MCP logs show connections but no detail; agent-worker completed with a diagnosis, but we don’t yet see explicit tool-call breadcrumbs for GitHub/Slack/Loki from the SDK path.

...

we need to test the github part where agent reads the code , finds the bug and also opens an issue.we also want to push the code to github .Also put .env in the gitgnore so that we dont push it to github and expose the credentials.

  "contentsAfterEdit": "# Credentials\n\nThe following credentials must be retrieved prior to running the agent. These credentials are required for the agent to function, allowing it to read Kubernetes logs, read GitHub files and make issues, and send messages in Slack.\n\n## 🔧 Common Credentials (Required for All Platforms)\n\n### Slack Configuration\n- **SLACK_BOT_TOKEN**: The token for the sre-agent Slack bot. If you haven't set up a Slack app yet, check out this [page](https://api.slack.com/quickstart) to create one.\n- **SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET**: The signing secret associated with the Slack sre-agent application.\n- **SLACK_TEAM_ID**: The ID of the Slack team where the agent operates.\n- **SLACK_CHANNEL_ID**: The specific Slack channel ID for the agent's responses.\n\n### GitHub Configuration\n- **GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN**: A GitHub personal access token with **repo-level read/write** access. Use a classic token with the &#96;repo&#96; scope (covers contents, issues, and pull requests) or a fine-grained token with read/write permissions for **Contents** and **Issues/Pull requests** on the target repository. Without these scopes GitHub will return &#96;Resource not accessible by personal access token&#96; when creating issues or pushing code.\n- **GITHUB_ORGANISATION**: Your GitHub organisation name (e.g., \"fuzzylabs\").\n- **GITHUB_REPO_NAME**: Your GitHub repository name (e.g., \"sre-agent\").\n- **PROJECT_ROOT**: The root directory path within your GitHub project where relevant code is located.\n\n### LLM Provider Configuration\n\n&gt; **Note**: You only need to configure **one** LLM provider. Choose either Anthropic or Google Gemini and provide the corresponding API key.\n\n- **PROVIDER**: The LLM provider name (e.g., \"anthropic\", \"google\").\n- **MODEL**: The specific model name to use (e.g., \"claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022\", \"gemini-1.5-pro\").\n\n**Choose one of the following:**\n- **ANTHROPIC_API_KEY**: An API key for Anthropic Claude models *(required if using Anthropic provider)*.\n- **GEMINI_API_KEY**: An API key for Google Gemini models *(required if using Google provider)*.\n\n### Security &amp; Access\n- **DEV_BEARER_TOKEN**: A bearer token (password) for developers to directly invoke the agent via the &#96;/diagnose&#96; endpoint. This can be any secure string you choose.\n- **HF_TOKEN**: The Hugging Face Hub access token. Ensure this has read access to https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-86M. Read [this article](https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/en/security-tokens) to set up this token.\n\n### Service Configuration\n- **SERVICES**: Comma-separated list of services running on your cluster that the agent should monitor (e.g., \"api,database,frontend\").\n- **TOOLS**: Comma-separated list of tools you want the agent to utilise (e.g., \"kubectl,helm,logs\").\n\n## ☁️ AWS-Specific Credentials\n\nRequired when running on AWS EKS:\n\n- **AWS_REGION**: Your AWS region (e.g., \"us-west-2\", \"eu-west-1\").\n- **AWS_ACCOUNT_ID**: Your AWS account ID (12-digit number).\n- **TARGET_EKS_CLUSTER_NAME**: The name of the target AWS EKS cluster the agent will interact with.\n\n### AWS Authentication Setup\nEnsure your AWS credentials are configured in &#96;~/.aws/credentials&#96;:\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n[default]\naws_access_key_id=YOUR_ACCESS_KEY\naws_secret_access_key=YOUR_SECRET_KEY\naws_session_token=YOUR_SESSION_TOKEN  # If using temporary credentials\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## 🌐 GCP-Specific Credentials\n\nRequired when running on Google Cloud GKE:\n\n- **CLOUDSDK_CORE_PROJECT**: Your GCP project ID.\n- **CLOUDSDK_COMPUTE_REGION**: Your GCP region (e.g., \"us-central1\", \"europe-west1\").\n- **TARGET_GKE_CLUSTER_NAME**: The name of the target GCP GKE cluster the agent will interact with.\n- **QUERY_TIMEOUT**: Timeout for queries in seconds (e.g., \"300\").\n\n### GCP Authentication Setup\nEnsure your GCP credentials are configured using the gcloud CLI:\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ngcloud auth login\ngcloud config set project YOUR_PROJECT_ID\ngcloud container clusters get-credentials YOUR_CLUSTER_NAME --region YOUR_REGION\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## 🚀 Quick Setup\n\nUse our interactive setup script to configure all credentials:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npython setup_credentials.py\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe script will:\n- Auto-detect your platform (AWS/GCP) or let you choose\n- Guide you through each credential with helpful prompts\n- Show current values (masked for security) and let you update them\n- Create your &#96;.env&#96; file automatically\n\n### Platform-Specific Setup\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# For AWS\npython setup_credenitals.py --platform aws\n\n# For GCP\npython setup_credenitals.py --platform gcp\n"
}

  "contentsAfterEdit": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"A script for setting up credentials for local development.\"\"\"\n\nimport argparse\nimport os\nfrom typing import Any, Optional\n\n\ndef mask_credential(value: str, mask_value: bool, show_chars: int = 3) -&gt; str:\n    \"\"\"Mask a credential value, showing only the first and last few characters.\"\"\"\n    if not value or not mask_value:\n        return value\n\n    # For short values, just mask everything\n    if len(value) &lt;= show_chars * 2:\n        return \"*\" * len(value)\n\n    return (\n        f\"{value[:show_chars]}{'*' * (len(value) - show_chars * 2)}\"\n        f\"{value[-show_chars:]}\"\n    )\n\n\ndef read_env_file(filename: str = \".env\") -&gt; dict[str, str]:\n    \"\"\"Read environment variables from a .env file.\"\"\"\n    env_vars: dict[str, str] = {}\n\n    if not os.path.exists(filename):\n        return env_vars\n\n    try:\n        with open(filename) as f:\n            for file_line in f:\n                line = file_line.strip()\n                if line and not line.startswith(\"#\") and \"=\" in line:\n                    key, value = line.split(\"=\", 1)\n                    env_vars[key.strip()] = value.strip()\n    except Exception as e:\n        print(f\"Error reading {filename}: {e}\")\n\n    return env_vars\n\n\ndef get_credential_config(platform: str) -&gt; dict[str, dict[str, Any]]:\n    \"\"\"Get the credential configuration for the specified platform.\n\n    Config structure:\n    - mask_value: When displaying existing values,\n        show masked (True) vs full value (False)\n    \"\"\"\n    # Common credentials for both platforms\n    common_creds = {\n        \"SLACK_BOT_TOKEN\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter your Slack Bot Token. If you haven't set up a Slack \"\n            \"app yet, check out this article https://api.slack.com/apps \"\n            \"to create one: \",\n            \"mask_value\": True,\n        },\n        \"SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter the signing secret associated with the Slack \"\n            \"&#96;sre-agent&#96; application: \",\n            \"mask_value\": True,\n        },\n        \"SLACK_TEAM_ID\": {\"prompt\": \"Enter your Slack Team ID: \", \"mask_value\": False},\n        \"SLACK_CHANNEL_ID\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter your Slack Channel ID: \",\n            \"mask_value\": False,\n        },\n        \"GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN\": {\n            \"prompt\": (\n                \"Enter your Github Personal Access Token (classic with &#96;repo&#96; scope \"\n                \"or fine-grained with read/write Contents + Issues/PRs on the target repo): \"\n            ),\n            \"mask_value\": True,\n        },\n        \"GITHUB_ORGANISATION\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter your Github organisation name: \",\n            \"mask_value\": False,\n        },\n        \"GITHUB_REPO_NAME\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter your Github repository name: \",\n            \"mask_value\": False,\n        },\n        \"PROJECT_ROOT\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter your Github project root directory: \",\n            \"mask_value\": False,\n        },\n        \"PROVIDER\": {\"prompt\": \"Enter your LLM provider name: \", \"mask_value\": False},\n        \"MODEL\": {\"prompt\": \"Enter your LLM model name: \", \"mask_value\": False},\n        \"GEMINI_API_KEY\": {\"prompt\": \"Enter your Gemini API Key: \", \"mask_value\": True},\n        \"ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter your Anthropic API Key: \",\n            \"mask_value\": True,\n        },\n        \"MAX_TOKENS\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Controls the maximum number of tokens the LLM can generate in \"\n            \"its response e.g. 10000: \",\n            \"mask_value\": False,\n        },\n        \"DEV_BEARER_TOKEN\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter a bearer token (password) for developers to \"\n            \"directly invoke the agent via the &#96;/diagnose&#96; endpoint. \"\n            \"(This can be anything): \",\n            \"mask_value\": True,\n        },\n        \"HF_TOKEN\": {\n            \"prompt\": \"Enter your Hugging Face API token, ensure this has read \"\n            \"access to https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/\"\n            \"Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-86M, read the following article \"\n            \"(https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/en/security-tokens) \"\n            \"to set up this token: \",\n            \"mask_value\": True,\n        },\n    }\n\n    if platform == \"aws\":\n        aws_specific = {\n            \"AWS_REGION\": {\"prompt\": \"Enter your AWS region: \", \"mask_value\": False},\n            \"AWS_ACCOUNT_ID\": {\n                \"prompt\": \"Enter your AWS account ID: \",\n                \"mask_value\": False,\n            },\n            \"TARGET_EKS_CLUSTER_NAME\": {\n                \"prompt\": \"Enter your target EKS cluster name (the cluster the \"\n                \"agent will interact with): \",\n                \"mask_value\": False,\n            },\n        }\n        return {**common_creds, **aws_specific}\n\n    elif platform == \"gcp\":\n        gcp_specific = {\n            \"QUERY_TIMEOUT\": {\n                \"prompt\": \"Enter your query timeout (e.g. 300): \",\n                \"mask_value\": False,\n            },\n            \"CLOUDSDK_CORE_PROJECT\": {\n                \"prompt\": \"Enter your GCP project ID: \",\n                \"mask_value\": False,\n            },\n            \"CLOUDSDK_COMPUTE_REGION\": {\n                \"prompt\": \"Enter your GCP region: \",\n                \"mask_value\": False,\n            },\n            \"TARGET_GKE_CLUSTER_NAME\": {\n                \"prompt\": \"Enter your target GKE cluster name (the cluster the \"\n                \"agent will interact with): \",\n                \"mask_value\": False,\n            },\n        }\n        return {**common_creds, **gcp_specific}\n\n    else:\n        raise ValueError(\n            f\"Unsupported platform: {platform}. Supported \"\n            \"platforms are 'aws' and 'gcp'.\"\n        )\n\n\ndef display_current_credentials(\n    credentials: dict[str, str], creds_config: dict[str, dict[str, Any]]\n) -&gt; None:\n    \"\"\"Display current credentials with appropriate masking based on config.\"\"\"\n    if not credentials:\n        print(\"No existing credentials found.\")\n        return\n\n    print(\"\\nCurrent credentials:\")\n    print(\"-\" * 50)\n\n    for key, value in credentials.items():\n        config = creds_config.get(key, {})\n        mask_value = config.get(\"mask_value\", True)  # Default to masking\n        masked_value = mask_credential(value, mask_value)\n        print(f\"{key}: {masked_value}\")\n\n    print(\"-\" * 50)\n\n\ndef get_credential_input(\n    prompt: str, current_value: Optional[str] = None, mask_value: bool = True\n) -&gt; str:\n    \"\"\"Get credential input from user, showing current value if it exists.\"\"\"\n    if current_value:\n        # Show the current value (masked or unmasked based on mask_value)\n        displayed_current = mask_credential(current_value, mask_value)\n        display_prompt = (\n            f\"{prompt}\\nCurrent value: {displayed_current}\\n\"\n            \"Press Enter to keep current value, or enter new value: \"\n        )\n    else:\n        display_prompt = prompt\n\n    # Use regular input for all inputs\n    new_value = input(display_prompt)\n\n    # If user pressed Enter and there's a current value, keep it\n    if not new_value and current_value:\n        return current_value\n\n    return new_value\n\n\ndef handle_comma_separated_input(\n    key: str, prompt: str, existing_creds: dict[str, str]\n) -&gt; str:\n    \"\"\"Handle input for comma-separated values like SERVICES and TOOLS.\"\"\"\n    current_value = existing_creds.get(key, \"\")\n    if current_value.startswith(\"['\") and current_value.endswith(\"']\"):\n        # Convert from string representation back to comma-separated\n        current_value = current_value[2:-2].replace(\"', '\", \",\")\n\n    user_input = input(\n        f\"{prompt}:\\nCurrent value: {current_value}\\n\"\n        \"Press Enter to keep current value, or enter new value: \"\n    )\n\n    if not user_input and current_value:\n        return existing_creds.get(key, \"\")\n    else:\n        return str(user_input.split(\",\")) if user_input else str([])\n\n\ndef get_platform_credentials(\n    platform: str, existing_creds: dict[str, str]\n) -&gt; dict[str, str]:\n    \"\"\"Get credentials for the specified platform.\"\"\"\n    print(f\"Setting up {platform.upper()} credentials...\")\n\n    credentials = {}\n    creds_config = get_credential_config(platform)\n\n    # Process standard credentials\n    for key, config in creds_config.items():\n        credentials[key] = get_credential_input(\n            config[\"prompt\"], existing_creds.get(key), config[\"mask_value\"]\n        )\n\n    # Handle special cases for comma-separated values\n    credentials[\"SERVICES\"] = handle_comma_separated_input(\n        \"SERVICES\",\n        \"Enter the services running on the cluster (comma-separated)\",\n        existing_creds,\n    )\n\n    credentials[\"TOOLS\"] = handle_comma_separated_input(\n        \"TOOLS\", \"Enter the tools you want to utilise (comma-separated)\", existing_creds\n    )\n\n    return credentials\n\n\ndef detect_platform_from_env(existing_creds: dict[str, str]) -&gt; Optional[str]:\n    \"\"\"Detect platform from existing environment variables.\"\"\"\n    aws_indicators = [\"AWS_REGION\", \"AWS_ACCOUNT_ID\", \"TARGET_EKS_CLUSTER_NAME\"]\n    gcp_indicators = [\n        \"CLOUDSDK_CORE_PROJECT\",\n        \"CLOUDSDK_COMPUTE_REGION\",\n        \"TARGET_GKE_CLUSTER_NAME\",\n    ]\n\n    aws_count = sum(1 for key in aws_indicators if key in existing_creds)\n    gcp_count = sum(1 for key in gcp_indicators if key in existing_creds)\n\n    if aws_count &gt; gcp_count:\n        return \"aws\"\n    elif gcp_count &gt; aws_count:\n        return \"gcp\"\n\n    return None\n\n\ndef create_env_file(credentials: dict[str, str], filename: str = \".env\") -&gt; None:\n    \"\"\"Create .env file with the provided credentials.\"\"\"\n    env_lines = [f\"{key}={value}\" for key, value in credentials.items()]\n\n    with open(filename, \"w\") as f:\n        f.write(\"\\n\".join(env_lines))\n\n    print(f\"{filename} file created successfully.\")\n\n\ndef main() -&gt; None:\n    \"\"\"Main function to set up credentials.\"\"\"\n    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=\"SRE Agent Credential Setup\")\n    parser.add_argument(\n        \"--platform\",\n        choices=[\"aws\", \"gcp\"],\n        help=\"Specify platform (aws/gcp) to skip platform selection\",\n    )\n\n    args = parser.parse_args()\n\n    print(\"=== SRE Agent Credential Setup ===\")\n    print(\"This script will help you set up credentials for running the agent locally.\")\n\n    # Read existing credentials\n    existing_creds = read_env_file()\n\n    # Ask for platform choice first to get the right config\n    platform = args.platform\n    if not platform:\n        detected_platform = detect_platform_from_env(existing_creds)\n        if detected_platform:\n            use_detected = (\n                input(\n                    f\"\\nDetected platform: \"\n                    f\"{detected_platform.upper()}. Use this? \"\n                    \"(y/n): \"\n                )\n                .lower()\n                .strip()\n            )\n            if use_detected in [\"y\", \"yes\"]:\n                platform = detected_platform\n\n        if not platform:\n            while True:\n                platform = (\n                    input(\"\\nWhich platform is your target cluster on? (aws/gcp): \")\n                    .lower()\n                    .strip()\n                )\n                if platform in [\"aws\", \"gcp\"]:\n                    break\n                print(\"Please enter 'aws' or 'gcp'\")\n\n    print(f\"\\nYou selected: {platform.upper()}\")\n\n    # Show existing credentials if any\n    if existing_creds:\n        creds_config = get_credential_config(platform)\n        display_current_credentials(existing_creds, creds_config)\n\n    # Get credentials based on platform\n    credentials = get_platform_credentials(platform, existing_creds)\n\n    # Create .env file\n    create_env_file(credentials)\n\n    print(\"\\n✅ Credentials saved to .env file!\")\n    print(\"\\n🚀 Next steps:\")\n    print(\"   Start the containers manually with:\")\n    if platform == \"aws\":\n        print(\"   docker compose -f compose.aws.yaml up\")\n    elif platform == \"gcp\":\n        print(\"   docker compose -f compose.gcp.yaml up\")\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()\n"
}

---

Added stronger protection for secrets and clearer GitHub setup. Root cause of the GitHub failure is almost certainly an under-scoped PAT; creating issues/pushing code needs full repo-level write permissions.

Changes made
- Expanded gitignore to cover all environment files so `.env` and variants stay out of git.  
```251:257:.gitignore
# Ignore Helm release artifacts
.release-name/

*values-secrets.yaml
# Environment files
.env
.env.*
```
- Documented the exact PAT scopes required (repo-level read/write for contents + issues/PRs) to avoid the “Resource not accessible by personal access token” error.  
```13:17:docs/credentials.md
### GitHub Configuration
- **GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN**: A GitHub personal access token with **repo-level read/write** access. Use a classic token with the `repo` scope (covers contents, issues, and pull requests) or a fine-grained token with read/write permissions for **Contents** and **Issues/Pull requests** on the target repository. Without these scopes GitHub will return `Resource not accessible by personal access token` when creating issues or pushing code.
- **GITHUB_ORGANISATION**: Your GitHub organisation name (e.g., "fuzzylabs").
- **GITHUB_REPO_NAME**: Your GitHub repository name (e.g., "sre-agent").
```
- Updated the interactive credential helper to remind you of the required PAT scopes.  
```69:74:setup_credentials.py
        "GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN": {
            "prompt": (
                "Enter your Github Personal Access Token (classic with `repo` scope "
                "or fine-grained with read/write Contents + Issues/PRs on the target repo): "
            ),
            "mask_value": True,
        },
```

What to do to retest the GitHub path (read code → find bug → open issue → push code)
- Generate a classic PAT with the `repo` scope (or fine-grained with read/write Contents + Issues/PRs on the target repo). Confirm it has access to the specific repo you’re targeting.
- Put it in your `.env` (now ignored) as `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN=...` alongside `GITHUB_ORGANISATION`, `GITHUB_REPO_NAME`, and `PROJECT_ROOT`.
- Bring up the stack and ensure the GitHub MCP server is healthy (e.g., `docker compose -f compose.local.yaml up github orchestrator prompt-server slack loki-mcp`).
- Trigger a diagnosis run so the agent exercises GitHub tools: hit the orchestrator’s `/diagnose` endpoint with a sample payload (or reuse your previous alert) and watch for tool events; with a valid token you should see issue creation succeed and pushes go through via `pushFiles`/`createOrUpdateFile`.
- If you want a quick manual check before the full loop, call the GitHub MCP server’s `createIssue` tool directly (via MCP client) or hit GitHub API with the same token; you should no longer see “Resource not accessible by personal access token”.

I didn’t run the services here since valid credentials are required; once the PAT is updated, rerunning the E2E should produce GitHub issue creation and code push without errors.

---

I can’t fully exercise Slack/GitHub without valid credentials, but here’s a concise test plan you can run locally to verify the end-to-end flow (agent diagnoses, posts to Slack, and uses GitHub to read code and open issues).

What you need (env)
- GitHub: `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` with repo read/write (Contents + Issues/PRs), `GITHUB_ORGANISATION`, `GITHUB_REPO_NAME`, `PROJECT_ROOT` (e.g., `aura-quiet-living`).
- Slack: `SLACK_BOT_TOKEN`, `SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET`, `SLACK_TEAM_ID`, `SLACK_CHANNEL_ID`.
- Orchestrator: `DEV_BEARER_TOKEN` for calling `/diagnose`.
- Optional: `ENABLED_SERVERS` to include github, slack, loki as needed (defaults include all).

Suggested local test (docker compose)
1) From repo root:
   - Ensure `.env` has the above values (keep it local; it’s gitignored).
   - Start stack (without rebuilding if already built):
     `docker compose -f compose.local.yaml up github slack loki-mcp prompt-server llm-server orchestrator`
2) Confirm health:
   - `docker compose ps` should show healthy for github, slack, loki-mcp, prompt-server, llm-server, orchestrator.
3) Trigger a diagnosis run:
   - `curl -X POST "http://localhost:8003/diagnose?service=demo-service" \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $DEV_BEARER_TOKEN" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d '{"log": "simulated error to trigger analysis"}'`
   - Expect the agent to loop: fetch prompt → possible Loki logs → GitHub reads → issue creation → Slack post.
4) Observe Slack:
   - Check `SLACK_CHANNEL_ID` in your workspace; confirm the final post arrives with diagnosis and any GitHub/issue info.
5) Inspect GitHub actions:
   - Issue creation: verify a new issue is opened in `GITHUB_REPO_NAME` with the diagnosis context.
   - Code access: check GitHub MCP logs for `GET /contents/...` and any `git/trees`/`git/commits` if push happened.
6) Check service logs (new terminal):
   - GitHub MCP: `docker compose logs -f github | grep -i github` (or just tail all)
   - Orchestrator: `docker compose logs -f orchestrator`
   - Slack MCP: `docker compose logs -f slack`
   Look for tool_call/tool_result events and ensure no `Resource not accessible by personal access token` errors.
7) If you want the agent to push code (via `pushFiles`/`createOrUpdateFile`), ensure:
   - PAT scopes include write on contents.
   - The branch (e.g., `main`) matches what you expect; set `branch` in requests if applicable.

If you want me to run these locally here, please provide valid Slack and GitHub credentials (PAT with repo write) and confirm I should bring up the compose stack. Otherwise, run the steps above and share logs/output; I’ll help debug.

Repo state (for awareness)
- Branch: `main` ahead of origin by 6 commits (merge completed).
- Staged: `.gitignore`, `docs/credentials.md`, `setup_credentials.py`.
- Unstaged: `.env` (kept local). No specstory files remain.

Next if you approve: I can start the stack, run the diagnose call, and report Slack + GitHub MCP logs and outcomes.

:siren: **INCIDENT REPORT: aura-backend**

**Alert**: E2E Test Alert - Synthetic alert for E2E test

---

## :brain: Chain of Thought

1. **Initial Observation**: The error logs show two distinct failure patterns:

   - Multiple database connection failures to inventory-db-01 with ConnectionTimeout errors, all exhausting retry attempts (5 retries)

   - A payment processing failure with GatewayTimeout error from Stripe provider

   - Corresponding HTTP 503 (Service Unavailable) responses for checkout requests during database connection issues

   - HTTP 500 (Internal Server Error) response for the payment timeout

2. **Hypothesis**: The service is experiencing multiple cascading failures:

   - Primary issue: Database connectivity problems to inventory-db-01 causing checkout failures (503 errors)

   - Secondary issue: Payment gateway timeout with Stripe causing transaction failures (500 errors)

   - The fault injection configuration shows db_connection_error: true and checkout_error_rate: 1, indicating this may be a chaos engineering test or intentional fault injection

3. **Evidence**:

   - Database failures: 10+ error logs showing database_connection_failed with ConnectionTimeout to inventory-db-01, all with retry_count: 5

   - Fault injection: Multiple fault_config_updated warnings showing db_connection_error: true and checkout_error_rate: 1

   - Payment failure: payment_processing_failed with error GatewayTimeout from Stripe provider

   - Impact: Multiple 503 responses during DB issues (02:46:34), one 500 response during payment timeout (09:14:07)

4. **Conclusion**: The root cause is intentional fault injection enabled via the /api/admin/fault endpoint

---

## :dart: Root Cause

**Fault injection configuration is enabled on the aura-backend service, causing artificial database connection failures (100% checkout error rate) and simulated database unavailability to inventory-db-01. Additionally, a payment gateway timeout occurred with Stripe.**

Confidence: 95%

---

## :clipboard: Runbook

**Immediate Mitigation:**

1. Disable fault injection by calling /api/admin/fault endpoint:

   

   curl -X POST http://aura-backend:4000/api/admin/fault -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"db_connection_error": false, "checkout_error_rate": 0, "checkout_latency_ms": 0}'

   

**Fix Root Cause:**

2. Investigate why fault injection is enabled (E2E test? Chaos engineering?)

3. Implement access controls on /api/admin/fault endpoint or remove from production

4. For payment timeout: Check Stripe API status and connectivity

**Verify Fix:**

5. Test checkout endpoint returns 200 status

6. Monitor logs for successful checkouts and absence of database errors

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Investigating invalid Host header issue**

The prompt-server rejects requests due to an invalid Host header "prompt-server:3001", suggesting `ALLOWED_HOSTS` or a related environment variable like `MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS` needs configuring to allow this host. Confidence scoring\n\"\"\"\n\nfrom functools import lru_cache\n\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI\nfrom mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP\nfrom utils.schemas import PromptServerConfig  # type: ignore\n\nmcp = FastMCP(\"sre-agent-prompt\")\n\nmcp.settings.host = \"0.0.0.0\"  # nosec B104 - Required for Docker container networking\nmcp.settings.port = 3001\n# Allow in-cluster service hostnames to pass host-header validation for SSE\nmcp.settings.transport_security.allowed_hosts.extend(\n    [\"prompt-server:*\", \"prompt-server:3001\", \"0.0.0.0:*\"]\n)\nmcp.settings.transport_security.allowed_origins.extend(\n    [\"http://prompt-server:*\", \"http://prompt-server:3001\", \"http://0.0.0.0:*\"]\n)\n\n\n@lru_cache\ndef _get_prompt_server_config() -&gt; PromptServerConfig:\n    return PromptServerConfig()\n\n\n@mcp.prompt()\ndef diagnose(service: str, slack_channel_id: str) -&gt; str:\n    \"\"\"Prompt the agent to perform a production-grade SRE investigation.\"\"\"\n    config = _get_prompt_server_config()\n    \n    return f\"\"\"## 🔍 INCIDENT INVESTIGATION: {service}\n\nYou are an expert Site Reliability Engineer investigating a production incident.\nFollow this structured, hypothesis-driven approach:\n\n---\n\n### STEP 1: GATHER INITIAL DATA\n\n1. Query error logs for &#96;{service}&#96; from the last 30 minutes using the Loki MCP tools\n2. Look specifically for:\n   - ERROR and WARN level logs\n   - Stack traces or exception messages\n   - Timing/latency information in logs\n   - Any metrics embedded in structured logs (e.g., db_pool_used, latency_ms)\n\n---\n\n### STEP 2: FORM HYPOTHESES\n\nBased on the logs, form 2-3 hypotheses about the root cause.\nConsider these common patterns:\n\n| Pattern | Log Indicators |\n|---------|----------------|\n| Database issue | \"connection pool\", \"timeout\", \"query slow\", db_pool metrics |\n| Memory issue | \"OOM\", \"heap\", \"memory\", gc_pause metrics |\n| External dependency | \"connection refused\", \"timeout\", service names |\n| Code bug | Stack traces, specific file:line references |\n| Configuration | \"config\", \"env\", recent timestamps near deploy |\n\nFor each hypothesis:\n- State it clearly\n- Identify what evidence would confirm or refute it\n- Rate initial confidence (0-100%)\n\n---\n\n### STEP 3: INVESTIGATE HYPOTHESES\n\nFor your top hypothesis:\n1. If logs reference specific files → Use GitHub MCP to fetch those files\n2. If logs show dependency issues → Query logs for related services\n3. If timing correlates with changes → Use GitHub to check recent commits\n\nSearch the codebase at &#96;{config.organisation}/{config.repo_name}&#96; under &#96;{config.project_root}&#96;.\n\n---\n\n### STEP 4: SYNTHESIZE FINDINGS\n\nUpdate your hypothesis confidence based on evidence gathered.\nIdentify the most likely root cause.\n\n---\n\n### STEP 5: GENERATE OUTPUT\n\nStructure your final response in this EXACT format:\n\n---\n\n## 🧠 Chain of Thought\n\n1. **Initial Observation**: [What the logs showed]\n2. **Hypothesis 1**: [Your first guess]\n   - Evidence: [What you found]\n   - Verdict: ✅ Confirmed / ❌ Refuted\n3. **Hypothesis 2**: [Alternative explanation]\n   - Evidence: [What you found]\n   - Verdict: ✅ Confirmed / ❌ Refuted\n4. **Conclusion**: [How you arrived at root cause]\n\n## 📊 Evidence Table\n\n| Evidence | Source | Finding |\n|----------|--------|---------|\n| [Log entry or metric] | [Loki/GitHub] | [What it shows] |\n| [Code snippet] | [File path] | [Why it's relevant] |\n\n## 🎯 Root Cause\n\n**[Clear, specific statement of the root cause]**\n\n**Confidence: [X]%**\n\n## ✅ Validation Steps\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Commands to verify this diagnosis\n[kubectl/curl/query command 1]\n[kubectl/curl/query command 2]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## 📋 Runbook\n\n### Immediate Mitigation\n1. [First action to stop the bleeding]\n\n### Fix Root Cause  \n2. [Action to fix the underlying issue]\n3. [Any code/config changes needed]\n\n### Verify Fix\n4. [How to confirm the fix worked]\n\n### Prevent Recurrence\n5. [What to add/change to prevent this in future]\n\n---\n\n## 📝 FINAL ACTIONS\n\n1. **Post to Slack**: Send the complete diagnosis above to channel &#96;{slack_channel_id}&#96;\n2. **Create GitHub Issue**: Create an issue in &#96;{config.organisation}/{config.repo_name}&#96; with:\n   - Title: &#96;[Incident] {service}: [Root cause summary]&#96;\n   - Body: The full diagnosis including runbook\n\n**IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS:**\n- Use ONLY the tools available to you\n- Query Loki for logs (not Kubernetes)\n- Be specific in your diagnosis - avoid vague statements\n- Include actual log snippets and code references as evidence\n- Create only ONE Slack message and ONE GitHub issue when ready\n\"\"\"\n\n\napp = FastAPI()\n\n\n@app.get(\"/health\")\ndef healthcheck() -&gt; dict[str, str]:\n    \"\"\"Health check endpoint for the firewall.\"\"\"\n    return {\"status\": \"healthy\"}\n\n\napp.mount(\"/\", mcp.sse_app())\n"

Tried to run the full local E2E; Slack/GitHub/Loki MCP connections are now working, but the run failed due to missing LLM credentials and an unreachable agent-worker. Summary of the run (run_id `55c9f374-f84c-4eaa-8a52-e4de529248f5`):

- Fixed prompt-server host-header issue and rebuilt. SSE connections to Slack, GitHub, Loki, and prompt-server now succeed.
- Agent-worker call failed immediately: `host.docker.internal:3005` unreachable (connection refused). The agent fell back to MCP flow.
- LLM call failed: `500 Server Error` from `llm-server:8000/generate` because `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is not set (compose warns it’s blank). Without a provider key the agent can’t produce a diagnosis or issue/slack post.
- Prompt template is still using placeholders (`changeme-org/changeme-repo`, `project_root=src`); GitHub issue creation and code fetch will target those unless envs are set.

Actions needed to get a successful Slack+GitHub E2E:
1) Populate `.env` with valid values (and keep it local):
   - `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=<your key>` (or set `PROVIDER`/`MODEL` appropriately).
   - `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` with repo read/write scopes, `GITHUB_ORGANISATION`, `GITHUB_REPO_NAME`, `PROJECT_ROOT=aura-quiet-living`.
   - Slack tokens are already working based on prior runs.
2) (Optional) If you want agent-worker (Claude SDK path) to run, bring up that service or point `agent_worker_url` to a reachable endpoint; otherwise it can be disabled, but it currently errors before the MCP loop.
3) Re-run: `docker compose -f compose.local.yaml up -d` then  
   `curl -X POST http://localhost:8003/diagnose -H "Authorization: Bearer $DEV_BEARER_TOKEN" -d "text=aura-backend"`  
   Check Slack channel and GitHub repo for the diagnosis/issue.
4) If the LLM call still fails, share the `llm-server` logs (`docker compose logs llm-server --tail=200`).

I won’t show `.env` contents or push anything; once the keys are in place, I can rerun the diagnose call and report Slack/GitHub results.

Got it—I won’t change anything. Based on `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, the intended primary path is the Claude Agent SDK (agent-worker), not an API-key LLM. The errors I saw were:

- Agent-worker unreachable: requests to `host.docker.internal:3005/diagnose` failed (connection refused / network unreachable). With SDK mode, that worker must be up and reachable on 3005.
- LLM server 500 was from the fallback self-hosted LLM path when the SDK call failed; it complained about missing API key because the SDK path wasn’t available, so it fell back.

To align with the documented flow: make sure the agent-worker (Claude SDK) service is running and reachable at `agent-worker:3005`/`host.docker.internal:3005`, so the orchestrator doesn’t fall back to the local LLM. No code or config changes were made.
